stories.headline: 11-year-old Centennial boy arrested on suspicion of murder in death of 5-year-old brother
stories.summary:
An 11-year-old boy was arrested Wednesday on suspicion of first-degree murder in the death of his 5-year-old brother at their home in the 5200 block of South Jericho Way in Centennial. Authorities responded to the residence at about 5:30 p.m. Tuesday and the older brother is now being held at the Marvin W. Foote Youth Services Center. The Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office said there is no ongoing danger to the community and that all individuals involved have been identified.

The 5-year-old victim was a kindergartener at Timberline Elementary, where Principal Mary Bowens sent a letter to parents Wednesday notifying them of the student's death and offering support resources. The cause of death remains under investigation, and sheriff's spokesman Anders Nelson declined to detail the nature of the injuries, citing the complexity of the early-stage investigation.

Under Colorado law, children as young as 10 can be criminally prosecuted, though those younger than 12 cannot be charged as adults and must proceed through juvenile court. Lawmakers have attempted in recent sessions to raise the minimum age of prosecution to 13, but those efforts have failed.

The school said its mental health team and district resources would be available to students and staff in the coming days. Arapahoe County investigators said the case is ongoing and complex.

stories.headline: Grand jury indicts suspect on tampering charges in death of Jax Gratton, Denver transgender woman
stories.summary:
A Jefferson County grand jury indicted Brandon David Mumma, 44, on two felony counts — tampering with a deceased human body and tampering with evidence — in connection with the death of Jax Gratton, a 34-year-old transgender woman from Denver. Gratton disappeared in April 2025 and her body was found in a narrow alley space between buildings on West Colfax Avenue in Lakewood in June 2025. The coroner ruled the cause and manner of death as undetermined.

The indictment, issued Monday, alleges Mumma removed Gratton's body, personal belongings, and other evidence from an office space at 9655 W. Colfax Ave. on April 16 to avoid detection by police. Prosecutors say Mumma and Gratton were in a consensual intimate relationship. Security footage shows Mumma leaving the building early that morning carrying trash bags that he disposed of in a nearby dumpster.

District Attorney Alexis King credited a months-long collaborative investigation between the Lakewood Police Department and the Denver Police Department. Lakewood police had previously drawn criticism for using Gratton's deadname and for a lack of transparency in the investigation, which helped spur a push to create a civilian oversight board for the department.

The case drew significant community attention and remains a touchstone for advocates of transgender rights and police accountability. The text of the two-count indictment had not been released as of Wednesday.

stories.headline: All 66 Colorado legislative Democrats sign letter urging Polis not to shorten Tina Peters' prison sentence
stories.summary:
Every Democratic member of the Colorado legislature — all 66 — signed a letter Wednesday urging Gov. Jared Polis not to grant clemency to former Mesa County Clerk Tina Peters, who is serving a nine-year sentence after being convicted in 2024 of orchestrating a security breach of her county's election system. The letter argues that clemency is reserved for those who have taken accountability for their crimes, and states that Peters has made no such efforts. Signatories warned that reducing her sentence would provide election conspiracy theorists a figurehead and signal that illegal activity around elections carries no consequences.

The letter escalates Democrats' near-universal disapproval of Polis' public posture on the case. Last week, Polis moved beyond hinting at early release to openly justifying the possibility on social media, contrasting Peters' nine-year sentence with the probation sentence received by former Democratic state Sen. Sonya Jaquez Lewis, who was convicted on similar charges.

Polis wrote on X that it was "not lost on me" that both women were convicted of the same felony — attempting to influence a public official — yet received vastly different sentences, calling for even application of justice. The governor's office did not respond to a request for comment on the Democratic lawmakers' letter.

Peters, 70, was found guilty of multiple charges including three counts of attempting to influence a public servant, conspiracy to commit criminal impersonation, and official misconduct. Her actions stemmed from conspiracy theories about the 2020 presidential election.

stories.headline: Federal judge criticizes ICE agents in Denver hearing for ignorance of court order on warrantless arrests
stories.summary:
U.S. District Court Judge R. Brooke Jackson sharply criticized Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents who testified in his Denver courtroom this week, saying they did not understand the requirements of his November order limiting warrantless arrests. None of the three deportation officers who testified Tuesday said they had received specific or formal training on how to conduct warrantless arrests in compliance with the order, which requires agents to fully document their justification before making such an arrest.

Jackson said the agents "didn't have a clue" and questioned whether any ICE agent was being properly trained. Because none of the arrest reports showed the required documentation, the judge said the agency was "by definition" not in compliance. ACLU of Colorado legal director Tim Macdonald said "the evidence of their noncompliance is extensive."

A senior ICE official, Denver assistant field office director Gregory Davies, testified that guidance from Washington conflicted with local direction, and that he had trained only 15 of roughly 200 Colorado and Wyoming deportation officers on the order — training that came only last month, after the ACLU raised compliance concerns. Davies also testified that four agents had been removed from street-level work in part due to documentation failures.

The hearing also addressed the January incident in which ICE agents left ace of spades "death cards" in the abandoned cars of arrested immigrants near Eagle-Vail. Davies confirmed those agents have been removed from field duty and placed on office assignment pending an ongoing investigation by ICE's Office of Professional Responsibility.

stories.headline: Avalanche lose 4-3 to Oilers as MacKinnon ejected in disputed goalie interference call
stories.summary:
Nathan MacKinnon was assessed a game misconduct and ejected late in the second period of Colorado's 4-3 loss to the Edmonton Oilers on Tuesday night at Ball Arena, following a disputed goalie interference ruling. MacKinnon made contact with Oilers goaltender Connor Ingram after being redirected by defenseman Darnell Nurse, but officials upheld the call after a lengthy review, resulting in a five-minute major penalty and ejection.

Coach Jared Bednar was visibly frustrated after the game, saying flatly "it's not a penalty" and arguing that MacKinnon had no chance of avoiding the goalie after Nurse ran into him. "I don't care if he's injured, not injured, it's a severe crash, not a severe crash. It's not a penalty," Bednar said. Colorado finished the game with just 10 forwards after Ross Colton was also injured during the contest.

Avalanche players including Cale Makar, Mackenzie Blackwood, and Nazem Kadri all questioned the severity of the call. Makar, who served as an on-ice spokesman with captain Gabe Landeskog out of the lineup, said MacKinnon has some of the best spatial awareness in the league and would not have cut toward the goalie intentionally.

The loss was a setback for the Avalanche, who lead the Central Division. MacKinnon's ejection was only the second game misconduct of his career. The team faces an immediate challenge of maintaining its division lead with key players either injured or facing potential supplemental discipline.

stories.headline: Nuggets rout Rockets 129-93, Murray leads with 30 as Denver rediscovers its rhythm
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets snapped a recent funk with a dominant 129-93 blowout of the Houston Rockets in a Western Conference matchup Wednesday night at Ball Arena, their most complete performance in weeks. Jamal Murray led all scorers with 30 points on 11-of-21 shooting, while Christian Braun added 19 points on eight-of-nine attempts, including three three-pointers. Nikola Jokic contributed 16 points, 12 rebounds, and 13 assists in just 30 minutes of action.

The win came after Denver had gone 6-7 in a stretch that included an embarrassing 39-point home loss to the New York Knicks last Friday. Coach David Adelman praised the team's third quarter as "arguably one of our best five quarters of the year" and credited improved defensive energy for sparking the offense. The victory also secured Denver the tiebreaker over Houston for future playoff seeding.

Houston star Kevin Durant was held largely in check, finishing with just 11 points on eight shot attempts. Denver's defense limited the Rockets to 40.9 percent shooting in the first quarter and consistently generated turnovers throughout.

With an eye toward the postseason, Adelman rested starters earlier than usual, as the team flew to San Antonio immediately after the game for a Thursday night matchup against the second-seeded Spurs. Braun, who missed 36 games with an ankle injury, appears to be approaching full health.

stories.headline: Broncos re-sign running back Jaleel Prentice to one-year deal
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have agreed to terms with running back Jaleel Prentice on a one-year contract, the team confirmed Wednesday. Prentice began the 2025 season on the practice squad before being elevated to the active roster in late September and going on to appear in all 17 regular-season games and both of Denver's playoff contests.

Prentice's role was primarily as a backfield blocker on offense and a contributor on special teams. The Broncos expect him to reprise those duties in 2026, providing depth and versatility in the backfield.

The signing continues Denver's activity during the NFL offseason roster-building period. Prentice had previously been with the organization and is now set for another year with the club after proving his value on both phases of the game.

stories.headline: Alterra Mountain Co. CEO Jared Smith to step down at end of 2025-26 ski season
stories.summary:
Jared Smith will step down as chief executive officer of Denver-based Alterra Mountain Co. at the conclusion of the current 2025-26 ski season, the company behind the Ikon Pass announced Tuesday evening. Smith has led Alterra since 2022. A leadership committee will temporarily oversee operations until a permanent replacement is named.

The announcement comes amid what Vail Resorts has described as the "most difficult weather environment in the Rockies we have ever seen" this season, though it is unclear whether conditions played a role in Smith's departure. Alterra operates a portfolio of ski resorts across North America and the Ikon Pass is one of the two dominant multi-resort season pass products in the industry.

A search for a new CEO is underway. The transition timeline aligns with the end of the ski season, allowing Smith to oversee operations through the conclusion of the current winter.

stories.headline: North Carolina joint venture acquires Littleton industrial building for $21 million
stories.summary:
A North Carolina-based joint venture has purchased a nearly 104,400-square-foot industrial building in Littleton for approximately $20.6 million, according to the Denver Business Journal. The transaction reflects continued out-of-state investor interest in the Denver metro area's industrial real estate market, which has remained tight in recent years.

The specific location of the property, identified as part of the Deer Creek Commerce Center, was not disclosed in full detail. Industrial properties in the Denver metro have attracted significant capital as demand for warehouse and distribution space has remained elevated.

The deal is among several recent transactions highlighting the strength of the local industrial sector even as broader commercial real estate markets face headwinds from higher interest rates.

stories.headline: United Airlines forecasts record spring break travel through Denver International Airport
stories.summary:
United Airlines, the dominant carrier at Denver International Airport, is projecting a record number of passengers during the spring break travel season, which runs from March 6 through April 27. Approximately 80,000 travelers are expected to pass through DIA each day during the peak period.

The forecast comes amid ongoing challenges at TSA, where staffing shortages have been exacerbated by a partial federal government shutdown that has left Transportation Security Administration officers working without full pay. Rising fuel prices are adding further strain to operations at airports nationwide.

Airlines and airport officials are preparing for the surge even as federal workforce uncertainties create potential bottlenecks at security checkpoints. DIA remains one of the busiest airports in the country and the anticipated record volume will test capacity across terminals.

stories.headline: Clear Creek Canyon Trail expands to 3.25 miles west of Golden, with more extensions planned
stories.summary:
A 1.5-mile extension of the Clear Creek Canyon Trail in Jefferson County Open Space, which opened in November, is now drawing Front Range visitors to a 3.25-mile paved path that winds through soaring canyon walls and past rushing rapids just west of Golden. The trail features four new bridges, 1,200 feet of viaduct-style elevated trail, multiple creek access points, and a new 42-space trailhead parking lot off U.S. 6.

The extension, known as the CCR segment in recognition of the historic Colorado Central Railroad that once ran through the canyon, is 10 feet wide, ADA compliant with a maximum 5 percent grade, and surfaced in rust-tinted concrete designed to harmonize with the canyon environment. Jeffco Open Space plows the trail after snowstorms to keep it accessible year-round.

A third segment adding another 1.5 miles upstream — the Huntsman's Rancho segment — is expected to open in late summer, bringing total trail length to 4.75 miles. Additional extensions are planned for future years. The two most recent segments cost $80 million to build due to complex engineering requirements, funded primarily by Jeffco Open Space with support from Great Outdoors Colorado and the Denver Regional Council of Governments.

The trail connects westward into the canyon and also links eastward through downtown Golden and along the Clear Creek Trail to the South Platte River in Adams County, forming part of the broader Peaks to Plains Trail corridor.

stories.headline: Colorado snowpack skimpy but dust-free this winter, scientists say
stories.summary:
Colorado's mountain snowpack is at its lowest levels since 1987 but has one notable silver lining: researchers have found no dust contamination in the snowpack this season, according to Jeff Derry of the Center for Snow and Avalanche Studies. Dust from arid regions in Arizona, New Mexico, and Utah is typically deposited on Colorado snowpack by winter storms, where it absorbs solar radiation and accelerates melting. The absence of major storm systems this year has meant little dust as well as little snow.

Derry conducted site visits from Rabbit Ears Pass near Steamboat Springs to Red Mountain Pass in southwestern Colorado in early March and found clean white snow at all monitoring locations. The irony, he noted, is that the same storms that bring dust also bring snow — the two arrive together.

Researchers have documented dust events every year since 2003, typically concentrated in March, April, and May, when about 80 percent of annual dust deposition occurs. In some years, as many as 12 separate dust events have been recorded. Scientists have found that heavy dust years cause earlier snowmelt, which reduces water entering streams and rivers by increasing plant moisture loss.

Derry said the recent storm that dropped 4 to 6 inches of snow across mountain areas could help delay melting. Whether the season ends up dust-free entirely remains to be seen, but researchers say there has never been a fully dust-free season in the 20-plus years of the program.

joke.joke: My kids think the dishwasher is a magical box where dirty dishes just disappear. Spoiler alert: I'm the magic.

topStory.headline: Iran Strikes Dubai Airport, Gulf Shipping as U.S.-Israeli Offensive Enters 12th Day; Calls Grow for Hormuz Escorts
topStory.summary:
Iran sharply escalated its military campaign on Wednesday, striking commercial vessels in the Persian Gulf and targeting Dubai's international airport with drones, as U.S. and Israeli warplanes carried out new waves of airstrikes on the Islamic Republic. Kuwaiti air defenses shot down eight Iranian drones, and Saudi Arabia intercepted five heading toward its Shaybah oil field. Senior Iranian officials warned of a prolonged "war of attrition" that could produce global economic chaos by throttling energy supplies from the region.

Israeli strikes on Lebanon have killed at least 634 people and injured more than 1,500 in less than ten days of fighting, with over 816,700 families registered as displaced with the Lebanese government. On Wednesday night, Israeli warplanes bombarded Beirut's southern suburbs and south Lebanon after Hezbollah launched a coordinated barrage of drones and rockets alongside Iranian missile strikes against Israel — the first time the two forces have synchronized attacks since the current conflict began. Israel's defense minister, Israel Katz, said the campaign would continue "without any time limit, as long as required, until we achieve all objectives."

Iranian officials acknowledged for the first time that Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei — who succeeded his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei after Khamenei was killed in the opening strikes of the U.S.-Israeli offensive — was wounded in the same attack that killed his father, mother, wife, and a son. Tehran's ambassador to Cyprus told the Guardian that Khamenei, 56, sustained injuries to his legs, hand, and arm and is believed to be hospitalized. He has not appeared publicly or issued any direct statement since the war began.

Israel also struck the Taleghan facility at the Parchin military complex, a site the Institute for Science and International Security assesses as linked to Iran's nuclear weapons development program. Iran had only recently rebuilt the facility after Israel destroyed it in 2024; Tehran has consistently denied pursuing nuclear weapons. Separately, the Kremlin confirmed that Moscow and Washington are in discussions about coordinating to stabilize energy markets disrupted by the conflict and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, though a Kremlin spokesman said it was "too early to speak about any effective cooperation."

The broader conflict is drawing intensifying international concern over energy security, with the Strait of Hormuz — through which a significant share of the world's oil transits — effectively closed to normal commercial traffic. Iran's parliamentary speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, warned that any U.S. or Israeli ground action against Iranian islands would cause Iran to "abandon all restraint" and threatened that "the Persian Gulf" would "run with the blood of invaders." NBC News reported that President Donald Trump has privately expressed interest in deploying a small contingent of U.S. troops to Iran and that seizure of Kharg Island, Iran's primary crude export terminal, has been under discussion, though no such action has been announced.
